text:
RETURN OFFICE VISIT — PRIMARY CARE
Patient: Margaret Okonkwo
Age: 52 years
Provider: Primary Care Internal Medicine

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Persistent left-sided throat discomfort and neck swelling, no improvement after antibiotic course. Patient returning for follow-up of ongoing symptoms.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Ms. Okonkwo is a 52-year-old Nigerian-American female, never-smoker, with no significant alcohol use, who presents today for a return visit approximately 5 weeks after her initial presentation to this office. She initially presented with a 6-week history (now totaling approximately 11 weeks of symptoms) of progressive left-sided throat discomfort and a persistent sensation of something being lodged in the throat. At that initial visit, she was found to have a firm, non-tender left level II cervical lymph node measuring approximately 2.5 cm on palpation. Given the appearance of pharyngitis and consideration of a reactive lymph node, she was empirically treated with a 10-day course of amoxicillin-clavulanate (875/125 mg BID).

She completed the full antibiotic course approximately 3.5 weeks ago. Today she returns reporting no meaningful improvement in any of her symptoms. The left-sided throat discomfort persists and, in her perception, has mildly worsened. She continues to note mild odynophagia — pain primarily with swallowing solids and, to a lesser extent, liquids. The sensation of a lump or fullness in the left throat remains unchanged. She also reports persistent left-sided referred otalgia, which she describes as a dull ache localized to the left external auditory canal and periauricular region. She has no recent upper respiratory infection symptoms, no fever, no chills, and no night sweats. She has not noticed any voice changes or hoarseness. She denies any trismus or difficulty opening her mouth. She has not noticed any bleeding from the throat or mouth. She has not had any involuntary weight loss that she is aware of, though she acknowledges that swallowing has been mildly uncomfortable and she has been self-limiting more chewy or hard foods. She denies any dyspnea, stridor, or sensation of airway compromise.

On repeat examination today, the left level II cervical lymph node remains palpable and, by her description, has not diminished in size — she feels it may actually be slightly larger than when she first noticed it. She has not identified any other new lumps in her neck or anywhere else.

She has no history of prior head or neck cancers and no family history of head and neck malignancy. She is a lifelong never-smoker and consumes no alcohol. She has no known exposures to radiation. She is not currently sexually active and has been with a single partner for many years, but her sexual history earlier in life is unknown in terms of HPV exposure risk.

Given the persistence of symptoms and failure to respond to antibiotics, a malignant etiology must now be more strongly considered. The presence of a firm, non-shrinking cervical lymph node in an adult — particularly at level II — that has persisted beyond a full antibiotic course for more than 4 weeks is a well-recognized indication for urgent otolaryngology referral and further workup.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
Positive: Left throat discomfort and fullness, mild odynophagia, referred left-sided otalgia, mild decrease in oral intake due to discomfort.
Negative: No fever, no chills, no drenching night sweats, no unintentional weight loss (patient unsure, but denies significant weight change), no hoarseness, no voice changes, no hemoptysis, no epistaxis, no oral bleeding, no trismus, no dyspnea, no stridor, no cough, no new masses elsewhere. No fatigue greater than baseline. Hypothyroidism symptoms: none (taking levothyroxine regularly, feels well from thyroid standpoint). GERD: mild, unchanged and well-controlled with dietary modification and occasional antacids.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY
1. Hypothyroidism — well-controlled on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily; last TSH within normal limits on most recent labs approximately 6 months ago.
2. Gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) — mild, managed with dietary modification; uses OTC antacids as needed.

---

SURGICAL HISTORY
No prior surgeries.

---

SOCIAL HISTORY
Ms. Okonkwo works as a high school English teacher; she has been on a brief leave of absence due to her symptoms and the associated discomfort with speaking for prolonged periods. She is a lifelong never-smoker. No alcohol use. She is of Nigerian-American descent. She lives with her family. She does not use recreational drugs.

---

FAMILY HISTORY
No family history of head and neck cancers. No known family history of thyroid cancer. Family history significant for hypertension in both parents; one sibling with type 2 diabetes. No known hereditary cancer syndromes.

---

ALLERGIES
NKDA (No Known Drug Allergies).

---

MEDICATIONS
1. Levothyroxine 75 mcg orally once daily (for hypothyroidism).
2. Calcium carbonate (Tums) as needed for GERD.
3. Amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg BID — completed 10-day course approximately 3–4 weeks ago; not currently taking.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
Vital Signs: Blood pressure 118/74 mmHg, heart rate 72 bpm (regular), respiratory rate 14 breaths/min, temperature 98.4°F (36.9°C), oxygen saturation 99% on room air, weight 68 kg (BMI approximately 24.1).

General: Ms. Okonkwo is a well-appearing, well-nourished 52-year-old woman in no acute distress. She is alert and oriented. She appears mildly anxious about her symptoms.

Head: Normocephalic, atraumatic. No scalp lesions.

Eyes: Pupils equal, round, and reactive to light. Sclerae non-icteric.

Ears: External auditory canals clear bilaterally. Tympanic membranes intact and without effusion bilaterally. No tenderness over the mastoid. Left-sided periauricular area without erythema or fluctuance.

Nose/Sinuses: No nasal obstruction. No tenderness over frontal or maxillary sinuses.

Oral Cavity and Oropharynx: Limited visualization of oropharynx on routine exam without fiber-optic scope — posterior pharynx and tonsillar pillars appear grossly without obvious erythema or exudate on indirect exam. Tonsils appear symmetric and non-enlarged on limited view. No gross oral mucosal lesions. No trismus — mouth opens fully. Teeth in acceptable repair; no obvious dental abscess or significant periodontal disease. No floor-of-mouth induration palpable. Tongue protrudes midline.

Neck: Supple. There is a firm, non-tender, discrete lymph node palpable in the left level II (upper jugular chain, posterior to the sternocleidomastoid, below the mandibular angle) position. On today's exam, the node measures approximately 2.5–3 cm by palpation — it appears at least unchanged, possibly marginally enlarged compared to the initial visit. The node feels firm and does not appear to have fluctuated or developed surface erythema suggesting infectious/suppurative adenitis. There is no overlying skin change. No tenderness to direct palpation. No contralateral cervical lymphadenopathy identified. No supraclavicular or infraclavicular adenopathy. Thyroid gland is not enlarged and without nodules on palpation.

Chest: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rhonchi, or crackles.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatosplenomegaly.

Extremities: No edema. No axillary or inguinal lymphadenopathy noted on brief survey.

Skin: No suspicious skin lesions of the scalp, face, or neck.

Neurologic: Cranial nerve II through XII grossly intact on screening exam. Facial movements symmetric. No facial palsy. Voice is clear without dysphonia.

---

LABORATORY AND DIAGNOSTIC DATA
No new laboratory studies drawn today. Review of prior labs from earlier this year:
- CBC (from routine annual visit approximately 7 months ago): Within normal limits; no leukocytosis, no anemia.
- TSH (approximately 6 months ago): 1.8 mIU/L — within normal range on levothyroxine 75 mcg.
- Comprehensive metabolic panel: within normal limits.
- No imaging has been performed to date.

A monospot test and CBC with differential were considered at the initial visit but deferred given the clinical appearance. Given failure to respond to antibiotics, laboratory workup is less critical than urgent imaging and specialist evaluation at this point.

---

IMPRESSION AND PLAN

1. PERSISTENT LEFT CERVICAL LYMPHADENOPATHY — FAILURE TO RESPOND TO ANTIBIOTICS, CONCERN FOR MALIGNANCY
Ms. Okonkwo presents with an 11-week history of progressive left-sided throat discomfort and a persistent firm level II cervical lymph node measuring approximately 2.5–3 cm that has not diminished following a full course of amoxicillin-clavulanate. The combination of: (a) a non-tender firm cervical lymph node at level II in an adult, (b) persistence and possible slight enlargement despite antibiotics, (c) associated ipsilateral referred otalgia — which is a well-recognized symptom of base-of-tongue and tonsillar pathology due to referred pain via the glossopharyngeal nerve (Arnold's nerve reflex) — and (d) prolonged odynophagia, raises significant concern for an oropharyngeal or hypopharyngeal malignancy with regional lymph node involvement.

Although the patient is a never-smoker and non-drinker — which reduces her traditional risk for squamous cell carcinoma — it is important to recognize that HPV-associated oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma is increasingly common in precisely this demographic: non-smoking, otherwise healthy, middle-aged adults. Level II lymphadenopathy in this setting is a classic presentation of oropharyngeal primary malignancy (base of tongue, tonsil).

The differential at this time includes:
   - Oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma (most concerning given the clinical picture)
   - Lymphoma (less likely but cannot be excluded given this node has not responded to antibiotics)
   - Reactive lymphadenopathy from a non-infectious inflammatory process
   - Infectious etiology (e.g., EBV mononucleosis, atypical mycobacterium, toxoplasmosis) — now less likely given antibiotic failure and duration
   - Thyroid malignancy with lateral neck metastasis — less likely given the clinically normal thyroid
   - Salivary gland neoplasm — possible but less likely

Plan:
   a. Urgent referral to Otolaryngology/Head and Neck Surgery placed today. The referral specifically notes: persistent 2.5–3 cm left level II cervical lymph node, failure to respond to antibiotics, associated ipsilateral otalgia and odynophagia, without obvious infectious source, in a 52-year-old never-smoking woman. The referral is marked URGENT, with the aim of an appointment within 1–2 weeks.
   b. CT scan of the neck with contrast is ordered today in anticipation of the otolaryngology workup, both to characterize the lymph node further and to evaluate the oropharynx, hypopharynx, and parapharyngeal space for a primary lesion. The imaging is ordered on an expedited basis.
   c. The patient is instructed to go directly to the Emergency Department if she develops any acute dyspnea, progressive dysphagia to liquids, stridor, or hemoptysis prior to her specialist appointment.
   d. No further empirical antibiotics prescribed at this time — there is no clinical indication for a second course and further antibiotic delay risks delaying diagnosis.

2. HYPOTHYROIDISM
   - Continuing levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. Will repeat TSH at the upcoming otolaryngology workup visit or sooner if symptoms change.

3. GERD
   - Stable and well-controlled. Continue as-needed antacids. Note: the GERD symptomatology has not changed and does not account for the current presentation.

---

PATIENT COUNSELING
The patient was informed that while there are benign explanations for a persistent cervical lymph node, the duration, firmness, and failure to respond to antibiotics make it medically necessary to pursue urgent specialist evaluation and imaging. She was told, transparently and compassionately, that a serious cause such as a lymphoma or a throat cancer must be ruled out. She was counseled to avoid alarm but to understand the importance of timely follow-through with the referral. She verbalized understanding and expressed willingness to follow up promptly with the otolaryngology referral. She was advised to monitor for any worsening symptoms and to call or return immediately if she develops worsening swallowing, difficulty breathing, changes in voice, or any other acute concerns.

The patient was reassured that pursuing this evaluation promptly is the appropriate course of action and that many such lymph nodes turn out to be benign — but that proper evaluation is essential.

Time spent: Approximately 30 minutes. More than half the visit time devoted to counseling and coordination of care.

Return to clinic: To be arranged based on specialist findings; patient instructed to follow up with this office if specialist referral is delayed beyond 2 weeks.

---

Electronically signed by attending primary care physician.
